sets.title: Perfective vs Imperfective
sets.description: Every Russian verb has aspect. The imperfective presents an action as ongoing, repeated, or without reference to completion. The perfective presents it as a completed whole — a result achieved.

pairs.sentence_b: Я прочитал книгу за два часа.
pairs.question: Which sentence tells us the book was finished?
pairs.explanation: "Читал" (imperfective) describes the reading as an ongoing activity — the focus is on the process, not the result. "Прочитал" (perfective) announces the book is done. The perfective has a built-in endpoint; the imperfective just describes time being spent.

pairs.question: Which sentence describes one specific instance that led to something else?
pairs.explanation: "Пил" (imperfective) marks a recurring daily habit — not one event but a pattern. "Выпил" (perfective) is a single act completed before leaving. The sequence "выпил и ушёл" requires perfective because the first event must be complete before the second begins.

pairs.question: Which sentence implies the letter now exists as a completed object?
pairs.explanation: "Писала" (imperfective) puts us inside the process — she was writing, mid-action. "Написала" (perfective) delivers the result: a finished letter. In Russian, the perfective creates the noun-like completion; the imperfective keeps you in the verb.

sets.title: Unidirectional vs Multidirectional Motion
sets.description: Russian has motion verb pairs: одно-направленные (one trip) vs. разно-направленные (habitual, many directions). The choice encodes whether motion is a single trip or a general pattern.

pairs.question: Which sentence tells us he goes to the shop regularly?
pairs.explanation: "Идёт" (unidirectional) describes a single trip in progress — he is on his way right now. "Ходит" (multidirectional) describes a habit or a round trip — he goes there (and comes back) regularly. One direction vs. many directions.
